coredata.dc:title: Highly photoluminescent N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAM) passivated carbon dots for multicolor bioimaging applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Min Cheol
Yu, Kwang Sik
Han, Seung Yun
Kim, Jwa-Jin
Lee, Jae Won
Lee, Nam Seob
Jeong, Yeong Gil
Kim, Do Kyung
coredata.dc:description: We report a highly luminescent Carbon Dots passivated by nitrogen rich molecules, i.e. N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAM), with a QY as high as ca. 94%, which is the highest yield until now. NIPAMM@C-Dots are synthesized directly from the precursors composed of CA, EDA and NIPAAM by hydrothermal reaction. The optical properties of in situ synthesized NIPAAM@C-Dots showed EX-dependent EM at high concentration and EX-independent EM at low concentration. In most cations and anions except Fe3+ and Bi3+ added to the aqueous solution, NIPAAM@C-Dots exhibit an extremely stable inert behavior. In addition, in order to verify its usefulness as a fluorescent marker, C6 glioblastoma cells and NIPAAM@C-Dots were cultured together and then applied by both confocal bioimaging and flow cytometry analysis. The role of NIPAAM present on or around the C-Dots surface can improve QY values and stability, resulting in reduced cytotoxicity and can be a strong candidate as an alternative to organic dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent
Nanoparticles
Multicolor imaging
Quantum yield

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-Dots by hydrothermal reaction
Multicolor imaging and labelling of NIPAMM@C-Dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research is financially supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (Grant No. NRF-2017R1A2B4005167 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAM) and NIPAAM@C-Dots (123E-160-5h) prepared by hydrothermal reaction.
TEM images of NIPAAM@C-Dots (123E-160-5h) prepared by hydrothermal reaction. (a) N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAM) molecules are formed as matrix and (b) amorphous shape of NIPAAM@C-Dots.
XPS survey scan of NIPAAM@C-Dots prepared by thermal decomposition.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and normalized PL spectra of the aqueous solution of NIPAMM@C-Dots (123E-160-5h). (b) Normalized PL spectra at EX WL from 320 nm to 560 nm and (c) their peak EM WL. (d) Photographs were taken under day light (left), day and UV light (middle) and UV light (365 nm).
(a) Up-converted EM spectra of NIPAAM@C-Dots (123E-160-5h) and (b) variation of maximum EM peaks and PL intensity excited at WL from 620 nm to 800 nm.

(a) Effect of pH values on the PL spectra of NIPAAM@C-Dots (123E-160-5h) in aqueous solution. (b) Variation of PL intensity depending on pH values. (c) Comparison of PL intensities of C-Dots in the presence of metal ions (50 μM M+ and C-Dots (O.D = 0.05) are incubated for 20 min). (d) Comparison of PL intensities of C-Dots in the presence of sodium anions (50 μM sodium anions and C-Dots (O.D = 0.05) are incubated for 1 h).

Confocal fluorescence images of C6 glioblastoma cells treated with NIPAAM@C-Dots (123E-160-5h). NIPAAM@C-Dots loaded C6 glioblastoma cells are incubated for 4 h and the labeled cells are imaged under fluorescence microscope. The cells are imaged under bright field (BF) and fluorescence (FL) mode with confocal laser-scanning microscope. Images acquired with green color, 488 nm EX, red color, 594 nm EX, and blue color, 358 nm EX. Images are representative of three independent experiments. Scale bar, 10 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Flow cytometric analysis of NIPAAM@C-Dots (123E-160-5h). After 4 h treatment of C6 glioblastoma cells with C-Dots, 10,000 events for each sample are estimated by flow cytometric analysis. C6 glioblastoma cells are analyzed for mean fluorescence signal by flow cytometry. The average MFI. SC, solvent control (D.W).
Different reaction conditions and their QY.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The QY was measured by slope method by using quinine sulfate as a reference.
(ABCE-XXX-HH; A: citric acid (mmol), B: NIPAAM (mmol), C: ethylenediamine (EDA, mmol)), E: means EDA, XXX: reaction temperature, and HH: reaction time).

head.ce:title: Highly photoluminescent N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAM) passivated carbon dots for multicolor bioimaging applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Anatomy, College of Medicine, Konyang University, Daejeon 302-718, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Anatomy, Brain Research Institute, School of Medicine, Chungnam National University, Daejeon 301-747, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Material Science and Engineering, College of Aviation, Jungwon University, Goesan-gun 28024, Chungbuk, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a highly luminescent Carbon Dots passivated by nitrogen rich molecules, i.e. N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAM), with a QY as high as ca. 94%, which is the highest yield until now. NIPAMM@C-Dots are synthesized directly from the precursors composed of CA, EDA and NIPAAM by hydrothermal reaction. The optical properties of in situ synthesized NIPAAM@C-Dots showed EX-dependent EM at high concentration and EX-independent EM at low concentration. In most cations and anions except Fe3+ and Bi3+ added to the aqueous solution, NIPAAM@C-Dots exhibit an extremely stable inert behavior. In addition, in order to verify its usefulness as a fluorescent marker, C6 glioblastoma cells and NIPAAM@C-Dots were cultured together and then applied by both confocal bioimaging and flow cytometry analysis. The role of NIPAAM present on or around the C-Dots surface can improve QY values and stability, resulting in reduced cytotoxicity and can be a strong candidate as an alternative to organic dyes.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are much more researched compared to other materials in comparison to their shorter research history. This is due to the fact that it has a narrow emission (EM) band, a high molar extinction coefficient, a high photoluminescence quantum yield, a symmetric EM of adjustable color, broad and strong absorption, reasonable stability, high brightness and light stability, large Stokes shift, and conjugation chemistries that can bind biomolecules [1,2]. Until now, many researchers have published a number of publications that focus on the implementation of QD NPs, particularly in cell marking and imaging applications for applications in the bio-related field [3]. In the case of organic fluorescent dyes, it is difficult to store the sample in the long term due to the photobleaching phenomenon in which the fluorescence efficiency rapidly decreases when exposed to UV or laser. However, QD NPs are relatively strong against photobleaching resulting in long-term sample storage. Moreover, confocal microscopy can be used to measure the specimens for long-time. QD-labeled growth factors [4] and individual membrane resident lipids [5] are able to target and interact with cells with minimal perturbation in the process being sensed. It is generally known that targeting to specific cells can be performed using receptor-ligand interactions by binding ligands that are over-expressed in specific cells to surface nanoparticles with a high surface-to-volume ratio. To efficiently target NP compartments for specific cell compartments, various biomolecules (eg, antibodies, aptamers, peptides, proteins, nucleic acids) can be conjugated and additional QD NPs can be incorporated into and out of the nanostructure [6]. QD NPs are available as a visible tracer of drug delivery system bodies due to its unique optical properties and has been extensively studied as imaging agents at the cellular and animal level [7,8]. Nevertheless, toxic heavy metals included in QD synthesis are causing serious problems with potential risks to human health and environmental pollution. The toxicity of QDs is regarded as a result of the release of toxic elements (e.g. Cd, Pb, In, Se, and Te etc.) from QDs [9]. Cadmium is known as a toxic element that can induce oxidative stress, DNA damage and apoptosis. The potential toxicity is still one of the major issues that limit the advances of QDs into clinical studies. To reduce the toxicity, inert shells are introduced on heavy metal based QDs such as ZnS [10] and polymers [11]. Carbon-based QDs are a new type of fluorescent material that can be applied in many applications due to superiority in water solubility, chemical inertness, low toxicity, ease of functionalization and resistance to photobleaching [12]. Due to these superior properties, many researchers have been developed synthesis routes including microwave assisted pyrolysis [13], electrochemical exfoliation [14], incomplete combustion oxidation [15,16], laser ablation [17], hydrothermal treatments [18], and plasma treatment [19] and intensive studies on photoluminescence (PL) properties. Especially, carbon dots (CDot) prepared by hydrothermal treatment of grass show a PL quantum yield (QY) of 6.2% [20] and bovine serum albumin and as carbon precursor under the passivation of decanediamine show a PL quantum yield of 11% [21]. The C-Dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of orange juice showed a PL quantum yield of 26% [22]. C-Dots's PL stabilizes the surface of the particles by surface passivation, which generates surface energy traps and hence higher emission intensities [23]. Therefore, passivation and doping on the surface of C-Dots is a popular approach to enhance the QY of C-Dots [24]. C-Dots passivated with PEG1500N synthesized by laser ablation methods had higher QY than those passivated with PPEI-EI [23]. When coated with ZnO/ZnS and PEG1500N, the QY of C-Dots (λex: 440 nm) can be increased to 45 and 50%, respectively [25]. The enhanced QY of Zn-based coating is attributed to a secondary, more effective surface passivation in combination with the PEG passivation agent [12].

In this work, passivation of N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAM) to C-Dots yields a QY of approximately 94%, which is the highest QY value so far. The NIPAMM@C-Dots are in-situ synthesized by hydrothermal reaction. By using mixtures of citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA) and NIPAAM with different ratios, we prepare nitrogen-containing C-Dots samples with various nitrogen concentrations. Briefly, the precursors, i.e. CA, EDA and NIPAAM, were dissolved in water and transferred to Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The mixture was heated at 160 °C for 5 h. In particular, NIPAAM was introduced as a surface protective agent with nitrogen along with the coating material, and polymers such as C-Dots/carbogenic dots were formed by condensation of CA with EDA.

Citric acid anhydrous (HOC(COOH)(CH2COOH)2, 99.9%), ethylenediamine (NH2CH2CH2NH2, 99.5%), iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3⋅6H2O, 98%), silver chloride (AgCl2, 99%) cadmium Chloride hydrate (CdCl2⋅xH2O 99.995%), sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 95–98%) and calcium chloride (CaCl2, 97%) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, U.S.A.). Quinine sulfate dihydrate (H2SO4⋅2H2O, 99%) were supplied by Acros. Lead(II) Acetate Trihydrate (C4H6O4Pb⋅3H2O, 99%), Manganese(II) chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O, 98%), Copper(II) Nitrate Trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2⋅3H2O, 99%) were supplied by Daejung. Magnessium sulfate heptahydrate (MgSO4⋅7H2O, 99.5%) and Potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5%) were is supplied by Junsei Chemicals (Chuo-Ku, Tokyo). Sodium Chloride (NaCl, 99.5%), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O, 99%), cobalt(II) sulfate heptahydrate (CoSO4·7H2O, 99%) and zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O, 98%) were purchased from Samchun chemicals (Kyunggido, South Korea). N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAM, 98%) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). All the chemicals was used without further purification. Triple distilled and deionized water is used throughout.

Excitation and emission spectra are examined by Fluorescence Spectrophotometer with SHIMADZU, RF-5301PC equipped with a 150 W xenon lamp. Luminescence is measured at an angle of 90° with respect to excitation light beam. The resulting particles are diluted with distilled water and measured at 25 °C. The relative fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of the C-Dots was measured according to the procedure of a reported method and calculated using the equation of Φx = ΦstdIxAstdη 2
x /IstdAxη 2
std . The optical densities were measured on Mecasys Optizen Pop UV–Vis spectrophotometer. In the equation, Ix and Istd are the fluorescence intensities of the C-Dots and the standard, and Ax and Astd are the optical densities of the C-Dots and the standard, respectively. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was selected as a standard with a quantum yield Φstd = 0.54 at 360 nm. ηx and ηstd are the refractive index of the C-Dots and the standard, respectively. The absorbencies of all the samples in 1.0 cm cuvette were kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength to minimize re-absorption effects.

The chemical composition of synthesized polymers was analyzed by an ALPHA FTIR with a Platinum attenuated total reflection (ATR) (Bruker Optics, USA). The samples were dried in oven at 50 °C overnight and grinded into fine powder with a motor and pestle prior to measurement. Spectra were measured with a resolution of 1 cm−1 and the wavenumber range was 500–4000 cm−1.

An FEI/Philips, Tecnai F30, 300 kV field emission source TEM instrument, equipped with STEM, an HAADF detector, and XEDS was used to perform TEM bright field high resolution imaging analysis. The software used was Tecnai G2 Digital Micrograph (DM) and Tecnai Imaging & Analysis (TIA). Samples were mounted on Quantifoil holey carbon grids by placing a few droplets of a methanolic suspension of the pigment, followed by air drying.

For XPS analysis, a drop of particle solution is placed on freshly cleaned (plasma cleaner) silicon oxide plates and left for 2 h to dry at ambient conditions. This step is repeated until a complete layer of C-Dots nanoparticles was formed on the substrate and the substrate Si signal was minimized during XPS analysis. XPS measurements are performed approximately 2 h after drying the sample. XPS measurements are performed using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe II Scanning XPS Microscope with a focused monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (117.40 eV) operating at 26.6 W. Beam diameter is set to 100 μm. The focused source is scanned over a rectangular 400 × 300 μm2 area of a sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-Dots by hydrothermal reaction
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical experimental procedure was as following: C-Dots were prepared by directly reacting the CA, EDA and NIPAAM. 2 mmol CA, 6 mmol EDA and 2 mmol NIPAAM were dissolved in 20 mL double distilled water (DDW) and located in 20 mL tubular vial and rubber stopper was inserted to vial followed by sealing with aluminum seal by vial clamper The sample was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL volume). Ensure that the rubber stopper was tightly contact with Teflon cap and further with outer stainless cap. The precursor loaded autoclave was heated hydrothermally at 160 and 200 °C for 5 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The tubular vial resists the pressure without rupturing up to 200 °C. The dependence of NIPAAM was measured by changing the molar ratio of 1–3 against CA. The reaction time was varied for 5 and 12 h. After heating the samples for predefined duration, the autoclave was allowed to cool at room temperature. The detailed experimental conditions and procedures are available in Table 1 .

The potential for bioimaging of C-Dots was tested using C6 glioblastoma cells. Approximately 1 × 105 C6 glioblastoma cells were deposited on each coverslip (diameter = 18 mm) to form a sparsely distributed layer of cells to ensure good exposure to C-Dots. C6 glioblastoma cells were cultured using the DMEM growth medium with 10% fetal bovine serum at 37 °C with 5% CO2. All the C6 glioblastoma cells were incubated until approximately 70% confluence was achieved. The mixture of carbon dots in the DMEM medium was added to each well. After 4 h of incubation in 5% CO2 at 37 °C, the C-Dots loaded C6 cells were washed twice with PBS to remove extracellular C-Dots and were then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde in PBS for 10 min. After mounting, fluorescence images were acquired using a confocal laser-scanning microscope (LSM 700; Zeiss).

C6 glioblastoma cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated FBS and antibiotics (100U/ml penicillin G and 100 mg/ml streptomycin) at 37 °C in 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere. In order to measure the cellular uptake of carbon dots quantitatively, C6 cells were seeded into 12-well plates at an initial density of 1 × 105 cells/well. Subsequently, cells were treated with different concentrations for 4 h. We removed the media and washed cells with PBS (pH 7.4) three times. Then the cells were trypsinized and resuspended in PBS (pH 7.4). The fluorescence of carbon dots in cells was evaluated by FACSCanto II flow cytometer, as directed by the manufacturer (Becton Dickinson). The mean fluorescence intensity of carbon dots were calculated for each sample. Flow cytometry data were collected using 10,000 cells and were analyzed using the FlowJo software (Tree Star, Inc.).

The detectors used were side scatter (SSC), forward scatter (FSC), and four different fluorescent detectors, with FL1 (FITC fluorescence emission), FL2 (PE fluorescence emission), FL3 (APC fluorescence emission) and FL4 (Alexa 700 fluorescence emission) set on different voltage. A negative control (solvent control, SC) was used for instrument set-up for the fluorescence channels.

(1) FITC fluorescence from the carbon dots then was detected using the FL1 channel, with a 488 nm (515–545 nm) blue laser. (2) PE fluorescence from the carbon dots was recorded in the FL2 channel with a 488 nm (564–606 nm) blue laser. (3) APC fluorescence from the carbon dots was recorded in the FL3 channel with a 633 nm (650–670 nm) laser. (4) Alexa 700 fluorescence from the carbon dots was recorded in the FL4 channel with a 660 nm (640–719 nm) red laser.

Data analysis was carried out with flowjo software obtained from BD Biosciences (Sydney, Australia). For data analysis, histograms were created by gating on the events falling within the defined region. Samples of carbon dot were analyzed on the histogram and the mean value recorded (MFI value).

Fig. 2 shows TEM image of as-prepared NIPAAM@C-Dots by hydrothermal reaction. NIPAAM@C-Dots are too small to investigate. Fig. 2(a) shows the polymeric N-isopropylacrylamide molecules are not fully decomposed to carbon dots because the decomposition temperature of organic molecules is higher than 350 °C by the date from TGA. Fig. 2(b) shows ambiguous fringe shape of several NIPAAM@C-Dots because it is amorphous phase. From the fringe shape, the particle size is in the range of around 2–3 nm. The surface compositional analysis of NIPAAM@C-Dots has been performed by the X-rays photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 3 shows XPS survey spectrum of NIPAAM@C-Dots nanoparticles. The detected elements were carbon (C, 56.8 wt%), oxygen (O, 29.8 wt%) and nitrogen (N, 13.4 wt%).

Fig. 4 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of NIPAMM@C-Dots (123E-160-5h). The absorption spectrum shows a peak centered at 243 nm corresponding to the π → π∗ of the aromatic sp2 domains. The strong peak appeared at 352 nm should be attributed to n-π∗ transition of multiconjugate CC of the C-Dots [26]. They have optimal excitation (EX) and EM wavelengths (WL) at 360 nm and 452 nm and show a blue emitting under hand held UV irradiator (365 nm). When PL spectra are measured, the NIPAMM@C-Dots need to be diluted due to the extremely high QY. The PL spectra (Fig. 4(b)) measured at the EX WL between 320 nm and 400 nm, they show almost fixed peak EM WL in the range of 451–455 nm and no more characteristic peaks are appeared when EX WL are increased. Thus, it could conclude that EX-independent PL behaviors but when PL spectra are measured at higher concentration (X 100) of NIPAMM@C-Dots, they show EX-independent PL behavior, which is coming from the broad size distribution of NIPAMM@C-Dots. However, the NIPAMM@C-Dots prepared in this work is highly monodispersed than other reported cases and we assume that minor size population will be less than 1% as EM spectra are shifted after increasing the concentration of the sample to 100 times. The normalized PL spectra shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c) have two stage EM gap between 420–440 and 480–500 nm due to the increasing the concentration of the sample 100 times at each stage to measure EX-dependent PL spectra. The maximum EM peaks are also shifted as increasing the concentration of the sample and EX WL. Interestingly, maximum peaks are varied in range of only few nm after increasing the concentration of the NIPAMM@C-Dots. In this case, the NIPAMM@C-Dots are more concentration-dependent than EX-dependent PL behaviors. In this approach, EM WL can be tuned by combination of optimal concentration of NIPAMM@C-Dots and EX WL.

In addition to the strong downconversion of PL properties, the obtained NIPAAM@C-Dots show strong upconversion fluoresce simultaneously (Fig. 5 (a)). The peak positions of PL spectra are almost unchanged in the EX WL from 620 nm to 800 nm, the upconversion fluoresce are also located at ca. λem = 452–454 nm, which is the same as downconversion peaks with a maximal EX WL at 740 nm. Fig. 6 (a) and (b) shows the variation of PL intensities of the NIPAAM@C-Dots influenced by pH. The PL intensity of the solution is obviously influenced in high H+ and OH− region, whereas the NIPAAM@C-Dots are reasonably constant PL intensities in the pH range of 4–11. These characteristic properties of NIPAAM@C-Dots are absolutely distinct ones comparing to semiconductor based quantum dots (QDs) and can be used in biosensing and bioimaging applications. Fig. 6(c) shows PL quenching effect of various metal ions (i.e. Ag+, K+, Na+, Co2+, M g2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Bi3+ and Fe3+) on NIPAAM@C-Dots, each at a concentration of 50 μM, are added into NIPAAM@C-Dots with an optical density of 0.05 adjusted by UV spectrophotometer. All the metal ions are stable on NIPAAM@C-Dots except Bi3+ and Fe3+. The PL of the NIPAAM@C-Dots is seriously quenched by Fe3+ ions due to the formation of complexes between carboxylic group of CA and Fe3+ ions. It is well reported that the quantitative analysis of piperazine derivatives chelated with iron (III) can be identified by spectrophotometric and potentiometric methods [27]. Fig. 6(d) shows the enhancement of PL intensity in the presence of anions, i.e. F−, Cl−, Br−, Cit−, NO3 −, SO2 −, SO3 2− and CO3 2−, of sodium salts. Interestingly, all the anion of sodium salts enhances the PL intensities except SO3 2−. Based on the results from the addition of cations and anions on NIPAMM@C-Dots in this work and other published work, the cations will not so seriously influence the PL intensities, whilst the anions in metal salts is more seriously influence PL intensities. Thus, stimulus of anions and cations can’t be directly compared to other published works. All the cases, cations, anions and their co-presence are the factors to stimulus on PL of NIPAMM@C-Dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor imaging and labelling of NIPAMM@C-Dots
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the wideness of dosing window and multicolor property of NIPAAM@C-Dots as a novel fluorescent label for bioimaging, C6 cells, which is the immortalized cell line of rat glioblastoma, are incubated with NIPAAM@C-Dots at 37 °C for 4 h and then imaged with confocal fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 7 , the C6 cells incubated with NIPAAM@C-Dots intensively illuminated by blue, green, and red colors under laser EX at 358, 488, and 594 nm. Although the exact subcellular localization site of the NIPAAM@C-Dots needed to be addressed by our further investigation, the NIPAAM@C-Dots are seen as granular inclusion in abundant cytoplasmic vacuole-like structures in C6 cells sparing the nucleus and the pattern of photoluminescence under all of EX WL totally merged. The fluorescence of NIPAAM@C-Dots is maintained without severe photo-bleaching even after 2 h irradiation (data is not shown). More interestingly, compared to the cases of given in 10×, no significant decrease in the intensities of the fluorescence is shown morphologically in C6 cell coincubated in 1× concentration of NIPAAM@C-Dots. Together with these results, we can suggest C-Dots might be a multicolored label for long-term cellular imaging with wide dosing window, allowing possessing the relatively good biocompatibility. Their robustness under photo-irradiation and chemical inertness compared with QDs are consistence with prominent photostability of C-Dots. These merits give N-doped C-Dots potential for imaging and sensing applications.

Flow cytometric analysis (Fig. 8 ) is adopted for another demonstration by quantitative approach for multicolor properties of NIPAAM@C-Dots at same EX WL. For these, fluorescent intensities of NIPAAM@C-Dots in C6 cells analyzed by fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) device at 488 nm laser EX. It is found that the fluorescence intensity of NIPAAM@C-Dots in C6 cells is increased by dose dependent manner. In detail, approximately 4 times high fluorescence intensity is seen in C6 cells coincubated with 10× compared by those with 1× NIPAAM@C-Dots. Most importantly, even at same EX WL (488 nm), both of fluorescence of fluorescein isothocyanate (FITC) and phycoerythrin (PE), which has EM WL with 515–545 and 564–606 nm respectively, clearly detected by this approach. Together with the results from morphologic study using confocal microscope, these results from quantitative evaluation might reinforce the potential of NIPAAM@C-Dots for being good candidates for bioimaging labels, largely due to their wide dosing window and multicolor property at same EX WL.

In summary, N-Isopropylacrylamide passivated C-Dots have been prepared from the precursors comprising CA, EDA and NIPAAM. The obtained NIPAAM@C-Dots exhibit QY as high as ca. 94% and EX dependent EM at high concentration, whist EX-independent EM at low concentration. The NIPAAM@C-Dots show extremely stable inert behaviors in most of cations and anions except Fe3+ and Bi3+. Moreover, the NIPAAM@C-Dots as a fluorescent labelling agent were incubated with C6 glioblstoma cells and applied by both in confocal bioimaging and flow cytometric analysis. NIPAAM on C-Dots will enhance the QY, improve the stability and reduce serious cytotoxicity, thus it could be a strong candidate as a substitute of organic dyes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (Grant No. NRF-2017R1A2B4005167).
